subsystem: Poppler PDF library — FontInfoScanner::scanFonts, the routine that walks a page/XObject resource dictionary to enumerate the fonts used by a document. It builds a GfxFontDict from the /Font sub-dictionary, records each distinct font into the caller's FontInfo list, and then recursively descends into nested /XObject and /Pattern resource dictionaries. It processes fully attacker-controlled PDF structure obtained through the XRef table.
provenance: Identified from the unmistakable Poppler API surface: the FontInfoScanner class and FontInfo type, GfxFontDict / GfxFont (Poppler's font-resource model), the XRef indirect-object resolver, the Object/Dict interface (lookupNF, fetch, isRef, getRef, isDict, getDict, dictGetLength, dictGetValNF, streamGetDict, isStream), and Ref / Ref::INVALID(). This is FontInfo.cc from Poppler (GPL-2.0-or-later). The header comment '/* sample: primevul_train_2369_fixed */' and the file name '...cwe674.cpp' indicate this is a hardened variant whose defensive mechanism is the visitedObjects cycle guard.
trust_boundary: Everything reachable through resDict and xrefA is untrusted: the PDF file (and hence every Dict, Object, Ref, stream, and the /Font, /XObject, /Pattern entries) is produced by whoever authored the document. An attacker fully controls the object graph — object numbers, indirect references, which entries are streams, and how resource dictionaries reference one another, including crafting cyclic or deeply-nested /XObject → /Resources chains. Nothing here has been authenticated; the only in-code defense against malicious graph shape is the visitedObjects set.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point. xrefA is the document's cross-reference table used to resolve indirect object references; resDict is a PDF resource dictionary (from a page or an XObject) whose fonts we want to enumerate; fontsList is the caller-owned output vector that collects heap-allocated FontInfo records. fonts and visitedObjects (used later) are member state of FontInfoScanner that persist across the recursive calls, so deduplication and cycle-tracking are shared over the whole descent rather than reset per level.

Two local pointers. gfxFontDict will own a heap-allocated GfxFontDict describing the fonts declared in this resource dictionary; font is a borrowed pointer to each individual GfxFont retrieved from that dictionary during iteration. Neither is initialized here except gfxFontDict which is set to nullptr on line 7 before any conditional assignment.

The /Font entry is looked up without following the indirect reference (lookupNF = 'no fetch'), returning a reference to the stored Object. Two shapes are handled: (a) the /Font value is an indirect reference (isRef): fetch() resolves it through the XRef table, and if the resolved object is a dictionary, a GfxFontDict is constructed from that dict, passing the reference r so GfxFontDict can resolve nested indirect font objects; (b) the /Font value is a directly-embedded (inline) dictionary: a GfxFontDict is built with a null Ref. If /Font is missing or is neither ref-to-dict nor inline dict, gfxFontDict stays null and the font-collection block is skipped. new GfxFontDict is the sole heap allocation of this owning pointer.

If a font dictionary was built, iterate its fonts by index up to getNumFonts(). getFont(i) returns a borrowed GfxFont*; the assignment-in-condition skips null slots. For each real font, *font->getID() dereferences the font's Ref* identity to obtain a Ref, and fontRef.num (the object number only) is used as the dedup key against the member fonts set. First-time numbers are recorded, and a new FontInfo(font, xrefA) is heap-allocated and pushed into fontsList (ownership transfers to the caller). After the loop, delete gfxFontDict releases the GfxFontDict allocated on line 13 or 16 — the new/delete pairing is correct and matched.

what_the_code_does.code:
const char *resTypes[] = { "XObject", "Pattern" };
  for (unsigned int resType = 0; resType < sizeof(resTypes) / sizeof(resTypes[0]); ++resType) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: The recursive-descent driver. resTypes is a fixed two-element array of the resource-dictionary keys whose values can themselves carry nested /Resources (form XObjects and tiling/shading patterns). The loop bound uses the idiomatic sizeof-array / sizeof-element to iterate exactly the two names; resType is unsigned and the count is 2, so there is no bound problem here.

For each of /XObject and /Pattern, lookup() resolves the sub-dictionary (following indirect refs). If it is a dictionary, iterate its entries by index; dictGetLength() gives the number of key/value pairs and dictGetValNF(i) returns the i-th value WITHOUT following an indirect reference, so the code can inspect whether the value is itself a Ref before resolving it. dictObjI is a const reference into objDict, valid while objDict lives.

This is the first half of the cycle-prevention fix. When the XObject/Pattern entry is an indirect reference, its object number r.num is checked against the member visitedObjects set. If already seen, the entry is skipped (continue), which stops the scanner from descending into the same object twice and thereby breaks reference cycles in the object graph. Otherwise the number is recorded before the object is fetched. Note the guard keys on r.num alone (object number), ignoring the generation number.

The entry is now resolved with fetch(). Only stream objects (form XObjects / pattern streams) carry an embedded stream dictionary with a nested /Resources, so non-streams are ignored. streamGetDict() returns the stream's dictionary, and lookup("Resources", &resourcesRef) both resolves the /Resources value and reports the indirect reference it came from via resourcesRef. This is the second half of the cycle guard: if the /Resources value was an indirect object (resourcesRef is not the sentinel Ref::INVALID()), its number is checked and recorded in visitedObjects so the same resources dictionary is not descended into twice. When /Resources is an inline dictionary, resourcesRef stays INVALID and no visited entry is added.

The recursion site. If the resolved /Resources object is a dictionary and — as a cheap immediate-parent guard — its underlying Dict* is not literally the same pointer as the current resDict, scanFonts calls itself on the nested resource dictionary, accumulating fonts into the same fontsList. Combined with the visitedObjects checks above, this is what turns an otherwise unbounded graph walk into a (mostly) bounded one: each indirectly-referenced object and each indirectly-referenced /Resources dictionary is descended into at most once for the lifetime of the scan.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Uncontrolled recursion depth (CWE-674) — residual after the cycle fix. visitedObjects prevents revisiting an object, so it eliminates infinite loops, but it does NOT bound recursion DEPTH: a PDF whose XObjects form a long non-cyclic CHAIN (object A's /Resources references B, B's references C, …, each a distinct object number) causes scanFonts to nest one native stack frame per link. With tens of thousands of distinct chained objects the C++ call stack is exhausted and the process crashes — a denial of service triggered purely by document structure. The immediate-parent check resObj.getDict() != resDict on line 64 only rejects a dictionary that points at itself by pointer identity; it does nothing about deep linear nesting. The safe invariant is an explicit recursion-depth cap (or conversion to an explicit worklist), independent of the visited-set which only deduplicates. Inline (non-ref) /Resources dictionaries additionally recurse on line 65 without ever touching visitedObjects, so they also add uncapped depth.

Potential NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476). font is known non-null (checked on line 20), but font->getID() returns a Ref* that the code dereferences unconditionally with the leading '*'. If a malformed embedded font can drive GfxFont::getID() to return nullptr, this dereference reads through a null pointer and crashes. The implementation must guarantee getID() is always non-null for any GfxFont that getFont() can return, or the caller must null-check before dereferencing. Latent: whether it is reachable depends on GfxFont's contract, which the shown code does not establish.

Potential NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476). streamGetDict() is called on obj2, guarded only by isStream() on line 52, and its return value is immediately used as the receiver of ->lookup(...) with no null check. If a crafted/degenerate stream object can make streamGetDict() return nullptr, this dereferences null and crashes on attacker-controlled input. The invariant the implementation must uphold is that every object for which isStream() is true has a non-null stream dictionary; if that is not guaranteed, a guard is required.

Cycle-guard keyed on object number only (CWE-20, robustness/logic). Both visitedObjects (lines 44/48/57/61) and the fonts dedup set (line 24) key on the object NUMBER (.num) and discard the generation number, effectively treating Ref as num-only. For the RECURSION guard this errs on the conservative side (it can only skip more, never less, so it cannot cause a missed cycle break), but for the fonts set it can cause two genuinely distinct fonts that happen to share an object number across generations to collapse into one, silently dropping a font from the output. This is a correctness/completeness weakness rather than a memory-safety hole; confirm whether Poppler's Ref semantics for this document class ever reuse a number with a different generation.

Exception-safety / raw-owning-pointer leak (CWE-401), latent and low. gfxFontDict is a raw owning pointer freed only by the single delete on line 30. If any call inside the loop throws — e.g. new FontInfo (line 25) on std::bad_alloc, push_back reallocation failure, or a throwing accessor — the delete is skipped and the GfxFontDict is leaked. The delete itself is correctly matched to new (not a new[]/delete or free mismatch), so this is a leak-on-exception, not corruption. Wrapping gfxFontDict in std::unique_ptr<GfxFontDict> would make the release exception-safe and remove the manual delete.

summary: This is the hardened (fixed) form of Poppler's FontInfoScanner::scanFonts: the member visitedObjects set added to the /XObject and /Pattern descent correctly breaks reference CYCLES in a malicious PDF object graph, closing the classic uncontrolled-recursion loop. The main residual risk is that the guard bounds revisits but not recursion DEPTH, so a deep non-cyclic chain of nested resource dictionaries can still exhaust the stack (CWE-674 DoS); secondary concerns are a couple of unchecked pointer dereferences (font->getID(), streamGetDict()) and dedup keyed on object number only.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Recursive entry point over untrusted PDF structure; central to the recursion-depth finding.
Raw owning pointer freed by a single delete; leak-on-exception path.
Borrowed pointer, null-checked at line 20; no ownership or bounds contract of its own.
Returns a reference to stored Object; type-checked before use (isRef/isDict); no length or buffer contract.
XRef resolution result is type-checked (isDict) before use; malformed refs return non-dict and are ignored.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: new GfxFontDict(xrefA, &r, obj2.getDict()) / new GfxFontDict(xrefA, nullptr, ...) (lines 13,16)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Heap allocation whose release is a manual delete; exception-safety concern (folded into the leak finding).
Index-bounded by getNumFonts(); getFont return is null-checked; int index is fine for realistic counts.
Unconditional dereference of getID() return; potential NULL deref.
Dedup on object number only ignores generation; can drop a distinct font (correctness).
Ownership transferred to caller's vector; allocation-throw contributes to the leak finding but no standalone issue.
Correctly matched new/delete (not new[]/free); only the exception path leaks, covered by the owning-pointer finding.
Fixed 2-element string-literal table; loop uses sizeof/sizeof idiom, no bound risk.
Correct array-count loop bound over a 2-element array; unsigned index safe.
Type-checked (isDict) before iteration; resolution of untrusted dict is guarded.
Index bounded by dictGetLength(); NF variant intentionally returns unresolved value for the isRef check.
Cycle-prevention key; correctly breaks cycles but keyed on number only (robustness) and does not bound depth.
Resolution type-checked before stream-dict access; non-streams ignored.
streamGetDict() return dereferenced without null check; potential NULL deref.
Sentinel comparison correctly distinguishes indirect vs inline /Resources; the visited-insert it gates is covered by the recursion finding.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: scanFonts(xrefA, resObj.getDict(), fontsList) recursion with resObj.getDict() != resDict guard (lines 64-65)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Self-recursion site; immediate-parent pointer check does not bound overall depth (primary finding).

findings.title: Unbounded recursion depth on deep resource nesting (stack-exhaustion DoS)
findings.anchor: scanFonts(xrefA, resObj.getDict(), fontsList) guarded only by visitedObjects (num-keyed) and immediate-parent pointer check
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion (bounds revisits, not depth)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0140_cwe674.cpp (scanFonts) plus the GfxFontDict/Object implementation (files not provided)
findings.invariant: Recursive descent through nested /XObject and /Pattern /Resources dictionaries must be bounded by an explicit depth (or nesting) limit, independent of the deduplication set — the visited-object set only prevents processing the same object twice, it does not cap how deep a valid non-cyclic chain may nest.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker crafts a PDF whose form-XObject /Resources dictionaries form a long linear chain of DISTINCT indirect objects (A→B→C→…, each unique so visitedObjects never fires). scanFonts recurses once per link, consuming a native stack frame each time; a chain of tens of thousands of objects exhausts the call stack and crashes the process, a document-driven denial of service. Inline /Resources dictionaries (resourcesRef == Ref::INVALID) recurse on line 65 without any visited bookkeeping at all, adding further uncapped depth.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no enclosing recursion-depth counter around scanFonts and that visitedObjects is the only descent guard; verify whether a maximum nesting depth is enforced anywhere in the call chain, and whether the platform stack size makes a multi-thousand-deep chain fatal.

findings.title: Unconditional dereference of GfxFont::getID() return (possible NULL deref)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0140_cwe674.cpp; contract lives in GfxFont::getID() implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: For any GfxFont returned by GfxFontDict::getFont(), getID() must return a non-null Ref*, or the code must null-check before dereferencing.
findings.failure_mode: font is non-null (line 20), but font->getID() is dereferenced with '*' without checking. If a malformed embedded font can drive getID() to return nullptr, dereferencing it reads through a null pointer and crashes on attacker-supplied input.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check GfxFont::getID(): does it ever return nullptr for a font that getFont() can hand back? If so, add a null guard before line 21.

findings.title: streamGetDict() result dereferenced without null check
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0140_cwe674.cpp; contract lives in Object::streamGetDict() (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every object for which isStream() is true must have a non-null stream dictionary, so that streamGetDict() can be dereferenced safely.
findings.failure_mode: Guarded only by isStream() on line 52, streamGetDict() is immediately used as the receiver of ->lookup(...). If a degenerate/crafted stream object can yield a null dictionary, this dereferences null and crashes on attacker-controlled input.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify Object::streamGetDict() cannot return nullptr when isStream() holds; otherwise guard the pointer before calling lookup().

findings.title: Object dedup/cycle key uses object number only (ignores generation)
findings.anchor: visitedObjects / fonts keyed on .num (r.num, resourcesRef.num, fontRef.num)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0140_cwe674.cpp (fonts at line 24, visitedObjects at lines 44/48/57/61)
findings.invariant: A PDF object identity is (number, generation); dedup keys that must not conflate distinct objects should key on the full Ref, not the number alone. For the cycle guard, num-only is acceptable (conservative); for the fonts set it must not merge distinct fonts.
findings.failure_mode: Two genuinely distinct fonts sharing an object number across generations collapse to one in the fonts set, silently dropping a font from fontsList (completeness bug, not memory safety). The visitedObjects guard is only made more conservative by num-only keying, so it cannot cause a missed cycle break.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether this document class ever reuses an object number with a different generation such that fonts dedup on .num would drop a real font; consider keying on the full Ref.

findings.title: Raw owning GfxFontDict pointer leaks if the collection loop throws
findings.anchor: gfxFontDict (new on lines 13/16, delete only on line 30)
findings.bug_class: Resource leak on exception (raw owning pointer, no RAII)
findings.invariant: An owning heap pointer must be released on every exit path; use RAII (std::unique_ptr<GfxFontDict>) so the destructor frees it regardless of exceptions.
findings.failure_mode: gfxFontDict is freed only by the single delete on line 30. If new FontInfo (line 25) throws std::bad_alloc, or push_back / an accessor throws, the delete is skipped and the GfxFontDict is leaked. The new/delete pairing itself is correct (no new[]/delete or free() mismatch), so this is a leak-on-exception, not allocator corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether any callee in the loop can throw in this build; if so, wrap gfxFontDict in std::unique_ptr to make release exception-safe.

audit_checklist: Is there ANY recursion-depth cap around FontInfoScanner::scanFonts, or is visitedObjects the only guard? A deep non-cyclic /XObject→/Resources chain must not be able to exhaust the stack.
Does the inline-/Resources path (resourcesRef == Ref::INVALID, line 56) also need depth accounting, since it recurses on line 65 without touching visitedObjects?
Can GfxFont::getID() (line 21) ever return nullptr for a font returned by getFont()? If so, the '*' dereference is a crash.
Can Object::streamGetDict() (line 54) return nullptr while isStream() is true? Add a null check if so.
Should the fonts dedup set (line 24) key on the full Ref (num+gen) rather than num alone to avoid dropping distinct fonts?
Is gfxFontDict release exception-safe? Replace the raw pointer + manual delete (lines 3/13/16/30) with std::unique_ptr<GfxFontDict>.
Confirm the immediate-parent guard resObj.getDict() != resDict (line 64) plus visitedObjects fully covers indirect cycles for all resource-graph shapes seen in real PDFs.
